title: The Rattrap by Selma Lagerlöf
subject: English

notes.heading: Theme
notes.text: Human goodness can reform a person. Compassion and dignity bring out the best in people.

summary:
The story is about a poor vagabond who earns a living by selling rattraps made from scrap wire. He is also a thief and lives a miserable, lonely life. He has a cynical philosophy: the whole world is a big rattrap. It offers baits like riches, food, shelter, and joy. Once someone is tempted, the trap closes and life becomes misery.

One evening he seeks shelter at a crofter’s cottage. The lonely old crofter welcomes him warmly, gives him porridge, plays cards with him, and even shows him 30 kronor earned by selling milk. The peddler steals the money the next morning. But then he gets lost in the forest, and he feels that the world-rattrap has caught him. Exhausted, he hears hammering and reaches Ramsjö Ironworks. The ironmaster mistakes him for Nils Olof, an old army comrade, and invites him home.

At the manor, the ironmaster’s daughter Edla sees that he is afraid and treats him with kindness and dignity, even after realizing he is not her father’s friend. She insists that he stay for Christmas Eve. That night the ironmaster discovers the theft and wants him thrown out, but Edla convinces her father to keep their promise of Christmas cheer.

Next morning, the peddler is gone. He leaves a rattrap as a Christmas gift for Edla, along with a note and the 30 kronor to be returned to the crofter. He signs as “Captain von Stahle,” because Edla treated him like a real captain. Her compassion gives him a chance to redeem himself.

qa.q: From where did the peddler get the idea of the world being a rattrap?
qa.a: The peddler was trudging along the road, completely absorbed in his own thoughts while looking at his wire rattraps. Suddenly, he was struck by a random thought that the entire world—with its lands, seas, cities, and villages—was nothing but a giant rattrap. He concluded that its sole purpose was to set alluring baits for people, tempting them with riches, joy, shelter, and food just like a trap uses cheese or pork.

qa.q: Why was he amused by this idea?
qa.a: The world had never been kind to the poor, homeless peddler, so it gave him an unusual, cynical joy to think ill of it in this manner. It became a cherished pastime during his dreary walks to think of people he knew who had already been caught in the dangerous trap, and of others who were still greedily circling around the bait.

qa.q: Did the peddler expect the kind of hospitality that he received from the crofter?
qa.a: No, the peddler did not expect such kindness at all. He was completely accustomed to meeting sour, unwelcoming faces whenever he knocked on doors to ask for shelter. Instead, the old crofter welcomed him warmly, offering him hot porridge, a big slice of tobacco, and a friendly game of cards until bedtime.

qa.q: Why was the crofter so talkative and friendly with the peddler?
qa.a: The crofter lived completely alone in his little cottage without a wife or a child. He was incredibly lonely and was genuinely happy to find someone to talk to and share his evening with. This isolation made him highly generous with his personal confidences, pouring his heart out to a total stranger.

qa.q: Why did he show the thirty kronor to the peddler?
qa.a: The crofter proudly shared that his extraordinary cow supported him in his old age and had earned him thirty kronor the previous month. When his guest seemed incredulous or doubtful of the claim, the old man got up, unhooked a leather pouch from the window frame, and physically held up the three wrinkled ten-kronor bills to convince him.

qa.q: Did the peddler respect the confidence reposed in him by the crofter?
qa.a: No, the peddler completely betrayed the crofter's trust. Although he left politely in the morning alongside his host, he returned just half an hour later. He smashed the cottage windowpane, stole the thirty kronor directly from the leather pouch, and walked away, choosing quick money over basic honesty.

qa.q: What made the peddler think that he had indeed fallen into a rattrap?
qa.a: After stealing the money, the peddler fled into a massive, confusing forest to avoid the public highway. As the winter darkness descended, he found the paths twisting strangely, trapping him in the same section of the woods. Exhausted and facing despair, he realized the thirty kronor was a bait, the forest was an impenetrable prison, and his own turn to be trapped had come.

qa.q: Why did the ironmaster speak kindly to the peddler and invite him home?
qa.a: The ironmaster visited his forge at night on a round of inspection and spotted the ragged peddler close to the furnace. Due to the dim, uncertain reflection of the fire and the peddler’s unkempt look, he mistakenly identified the tramp as his old regimental comrade, Nils Olof (Captain von Stahle), and wanted to offer him Christmas hospitality.

qa.q: Why did the peddler decline the invitation?
qa.a: The peddler knew he was not the captain, but he initially kept quiet hoping to receive a few kronor. However, when the ironmaster insisted on taking him to his grand manor house, the peddler felt alarmed. Knowing he had stolen thirty kronor, he felt that entering the manor would be like throwing himself voluntarily into a lion's den.

qa.q: What made the peddler accept Edla Willmansson’s invitation?
qa.a: Edla spoke to the frightened peddler with deep compassion, kindness, and sincerity. She noticed his terror and quickly assured him that he would be allowed to leave their home just as freely as he arrived, asking only for his company for Christmas Eve. Her warm manner inspired absolute confidence in him, making him accept the invitation.

qa.q: What doubts did Edla have about the peddler?
qa.a: The moment Edla lifted his hat in the forge, she noticed his intense, defensive fear. She instantly thought that the man had either stolen something valuable or escaped from a prison. Later at breakfast, she noted that nothing about his demeanor or speech suggested he had ever been an educated military man.

qa.q: When did the ironmaster realise his mistake?
qa.a: The ironmaster realized his mistake the following morning in broad daylight. After the valet had bathed, shaved, hair-cut, and dressed the peddler in a well-fitting clean suit, the tramp stood in the dining room for breakfast. Without the furnace shadows, the ironmaster saw immediately that he was a total stranger and not his army comrade.

qa.q: What did the peddler say in his defence when it was clear that he was not the person the ironmaster had thought he was?
qa.a: The peddler argued that he never tried to deceive them or pretend to be a captain; he had merely begged to be allowed to sleep safely in the forge. He then struck the table and boldly delivered his philosophy, warning the ironmaster that the entire world is a big rattrap, and a day might come when the ironmaster himself would get caught by looking for a big piece of pork.

qa.q: Why did Edla still entertain the peddler even after she knew the truth about him?
qa.a: Edla felt deep empathy for the poor vagabond who spent his entire year being chased away, unwelcome, and living in fear of arrest. She insisted that since they had personally invited him and promised him Christmas cheer, it would be cruel to chase him away. She simply wanted him to enjoy just one single day of peace and safety in the entire year.

qa.q: Why was Edla happy to see the gift left by the peddler?
qa.a: Edla was dejected after learning at church that their guest was a thief who had robbed the old crofter. However, when she opened his parting package, she found his gift containing a tiny rattrap, the stolen thirty kronor, and a beautiful letter. She cried with joy because the gift proved that her unconditional kindness had successfully redeemed his soul.

qa.q: Why did the peddler sign himself as Captain von Stahle?
qa.a: Edla had treated the low vagabond with the high respect and dignity due to a real captain all day long. This elevated treatment inspired him to act with equal nobility in return. He signed as Captain von Stahle to show that her trust had elevated his character, giving him the inner strength to break free from his criminal mindset.

qa.q: How does the peddler interpret the acts of kindness and hospitality shown by the crofter, the ironmaster and his daughter?
qa.a: The peddler initially views the hospitality of the crofter and the ironmaster through a lens of suspicion and opportunism; he exploits the crofter's trust to steal money and considers the ironmaster’s invite a trap. However, Edla’s genuine, selfless compassion alters his perspective completely. He interprets her kindness as a pure force that rescues him from the world's deceptive snare, treating her grace as an opportunity to wash away his sins.

qa.q: What are the instances in the story that show that the character of the ironmaster is different from that of his daughter in many ways?
qa.a: The ironmaster is impulsive, status-conscious, and quick to judge; he invites the tramp based only on perceived status and instantly wants to call the sheriff upon realizing his error. In contrast, Edla is perceptive, deeply compassionate, and independent. She notices his criminal fear early on, looks past his true identity to see his core humanity, and stands up to her father to protect a helpless stranger.

qa.q: The story has many instances of unexpected reactions from the characters to others’ behaviour. Pick out instances of these surprises.
qa.a: The story is full of ironies and surprising turnarounds: the lonely crofter welcomes a ragged stranger with immense warmth instead of a typical sour face; the peddler repays this kindness by robbing his host; the ironmaster aggressively mistakes a vagabond for his elite friend but threatens arrest later; and most surprisingly, Edla fiercely defends a confessed liar, which inspires the thief to return the stolen cash.

qa.q: What made the peddler finally change his ways?
qa.a: The peddler changed his ways due to the unconditional love, absolute trust, and high dignity shown to him by Edla Willmansson. Even after his real identity was exposed, she chose to treat him with the respect of a captain. This profound experience awakened his latent essential goodness, giving him the self-respect needed to rise above his miserable life of thievery.

qa.q: How does the metaphor of the rattrap serve to highlight the human predicament?
qa.a: The metaphor highlights how human beings are easily enslaved by material desires. The world offers baits like wealth, power, luxury, and comfort. The moment a person succumbs to greed and reaches out to touch these temptations, the metaphorical trap snaps shut, plunging their life into an inescapable cycle of spiritual darkness, isolation, and moral corruption.

qa.q: The peddler comes out as a person with a subtle sense of humour. How does this serve in lightening the seriousness of the theme of the story and also endear him to us?
qa.a: The peddler uses his unique rattrap philosophy as a humorous shield to cope with his miserable condition. His witty response when caught by the ironmaster—warning him that he too might get trapped by a piece of pork—makes even the serious ironmaster laugh and drop his anger. This clever self-awareness and philosophical wit make him a deeply human and endearing protagonist rather than a common villain.
